---
title: "Simple Workflows on Lonestar6"
subtitle: "A 1-hour beginner workshop"
author: "Richard Herrington"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    code-overflow: wrap
    incremental: false
    preview-links: auto
    controls: true
    navigation-mode: linear
title-slide-attributes: 
  data-background-image: unt.png
  data-background-size: 23%
  data-background-position: 2% 2%
---

## Welcome

**Goal:** get from login to a successfully submitted SLURM job.

By the end of this workshop, participants will be able to:

- explain login nodes vs compute nodes
- navigate to a working directory
- load a module
- write a simple SLURM script
- submit with `sbatch`
- inspect job output

::: notes
Open by telling participants this is not a survey of all HPC topics.
It is one coherent workflow.
Success for today = "I submitted a real job and understand what happened."
Suggested time: 3 minutes.
:::

## Workshop map

| Time | Focus |
|---|---|
| 0–10 min | What HPC is and how the cluster works |
| 10–20 min | Minimal Linux skills for this workshop |
| 20–28 min | Modules and environments |
| 28–48 min | Writing and submitting a SLURM job |
| 48–56 min | Debugging common beginner mistakes |
| 56–60 min | Wrap-up and next steps |

::: notes
This slide sets expectations and keeps the session paced.
If time gets tight, shorten the Linux section and keep the SLURM section intact.
:::

## What is HPC?

High-performance computing helps when:

- one machine is too slow
- one machine does not have enough memory
- work can be split across many cores or nodes

In practice, HPC usually means:

- shared systems
- queued work
- command-line workflows
- resource-aware computing

::: notes
Keep this concrete. Avoid a long definition.
The point is to motivate why the scheduler exists.
:::

## Mental model: the cluster

```text
Your laptop
   |
   | ssh
   v
Login node  ---> prepare files, edit scripts, load modules, submit jobs
   |
   | sbatch
   v
SLURM scheduler ---> decides when and where your work runs
   |
   v
Compute node(s) ---> actual execution
```

**Key idea:** most real computation happens on compute nodes, not on login nodes.

::: notes
This is the anchor slide for the whole workshop.
Come back to this diagram several times.
:::

## Login nodes vs compute nodes

| Login nodes | Compute nodes |
|---|---|
| edit files | run jobs |
| inspect directories | use allocated resources |
| move data | execute serious computation |
| load modules | run batch or interactive workloads |
| submit jobs | produce output |

**Rule of thumb:** do setup on the login node; do computation on compute nodes.

::: notes
Ask: "What would be a bad use of a login node?"
Good answers: long jobs, heavy analysis, training, large parallel runs.
:::

## The smallest Linux toolkit we need today

Commands we will actually use:

```bash
pwd
ls
cd
mkdir
nano
chmod +x
```

We are not trying to learn all of Linux today.
We are learning just enough to complete one real workflow.

::: notes
Say explicitly that beginners do not need to master Linux before doing useful HPC work.
This lowers anxiety.
:::

## Demo: make a working directory

```bash
mkdir hpc_workshop
cd hpc_workshop
pwd
ls
```

Questions to ask:

- Where am I?
- What files are here?
- What directory will my job run from?

::: notes
Do this live.
Emphasize that many beginner errors are really directory errors.
Suggested time: 4 minutes.
:::

## Permissions matter

A common pattern:

```bash
nano hello.sh
chmod +x hello.sh
./hello.sh
```

Operational point:

- a script may exist but not be executable
- `chmod +x` fixes that for simple cases
- `ls -l` helps diagnose it

::: notes
Do not go deep into Unix permissions.
Teach only the operational point: if a script will not run, check permissions.
:::

## Quick permission check

Example:

```bash
ls -l hello.sh
```

What to look for:

- can you see the file?
- does it have `x` in the permission string?
- are you in the right directory?

::: notes
This slide replaces a crowded explanation slide.
Keep it quick and practical.
:::

## Modules: software is part of the environment

On clusters, software is often not globally available in the way users expect on a laptop.

Instead, we load what we need into the current environment.

Common commands:

```bash
module avail
module load gcc
module list
which gcc
gcc --version
```

::: notes
This is a mindset shift slide.
The conceptual point matters more than the exact compiler example.
:::

## Demo: load a compiler module

```bash
module load gcc
which gcc
gcc --version
```

What this demonstrates:

- the environment changed
- a command became available
- the software path is now visible

::: notes
If module names differ on the actual system, adapt live.
The important move is: load something, verify it, show that environment matters.
Suggested time: 5 minutes.
:::

## Why a scheduler exists

Without a scheduler:

- users compete directly for resources
- large jobs can overwhelm shared systems
- there is no fair queueing or accounting

With SLURM:

- users request resources
- jobs wait in a queue if needed
- work runs on compute nodes when resources are available

**Mental model:** a SLURM job script is a contract:

> Here is what I want to run, and here are the resources I need.

::: notes
This is the transition from shell work to real HPC work.
:::

## Anatomy of a SLURM script

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J hello_job
#SBATCH -N 1
#SBATCH -n 1
#SBATCH -t 00:01:00
#SBATCH -p normal

hostname
date
```

Two parts:

1. resource requests and metadata
2. commands to execute

::: notes
Read the script top to bottom.
Do not assume students know what a shebang is.
:::

## What the directives mean

| Line | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `#SBATCH -J hello_job` | readable job name |
| `#SBATCH -N 1` | number of nodes |
| `#SBATCH -n 1` | number of tasks |
| `#SBATCH -t 00:01:00` | wall time limit |
| `#SBATCH -p normal` | partition or queue |

Then the shell commands run on the allocated compute resource.

::: notes
Keep this slow and explicit.
New users often confuse tasks, nodes, and cores.
For this workshop, do not overcomplicate the distinction.
:::

## Our first job script

Save as `job.slurm`:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J hello_job
#SBATCH -N 1
#SBATCH -n 1
#SBATCH -t 00:01:00
#SBATCH -p normal

echo "Hello from compute node"
hostname
date
pwd
```

::: notes
If you want, add `sleep 15` so the queue state is easier to observe live.
:::

## Why this is a good first job

It is:

- short
- safe
- easy to explain
- easy to debug

It also shows:

- where the job ran
- when it ran
- which directory it used

::: notes
This used to be crowded onto the script slide.
Splitting it keeps the code readable.
:::

## Submit and monitor

Submit the job:

```bash
sbatch job.slurm
```

Monitor the queue:

```bash
squeue -u $USER
```

::: notes
Explain that `sbatch` submits the job; it does not run it immediately on the login node.
That misconception is extremely common.
:::

## Inspect completed work

Read the output file:

```bash
cat slurm-*.out
```

Optional accounting check:

```bash
sacct
```

Think of the output file as your first debugging checkpoint.

::: notes
This split gives the commands room to breathe.
:::

## What the output tells us

A typical output file might show:

```text
Hello from compute node
c123-456
Tue Apr 15 10:31:42 CDT 2026
/home/username/hpc_workshop
```

Interpretation:

- the script ran successfully
- the hostname identifies the execution host
- the date confirms runtime
- `pwd` confirms the working directory

::: notes
Connect this back to the earlier mental model slide.
"You were on the login node when you submitted; the work ran somewhere else."
:::

## Very common beginner mistakes I

1. **Wrong directory**
   - fix with `pwd` and `ls`
2. **Script name typo**
   - check `ls`
3. **Job is pending**
   - use `squeue -u $USER`

::: notes
This is where the workshop becomes realistic.
Students trust the material more when it includes failure modes.
:::

## Very common beginner mistakes II

4. **Bad command inside the script**
   - inspect `slurm-*.out`
5. **Forgot to load needed software**
   - add `module load ...` in the job script when needed

**Fast triage order:**

1. `pwd`
2. `ls`
3. `squeue -u $USER`
4. `cat slurm-*.out`

::: notes
This split should keep the slide from running off screen.
It also gives you a simple troubleshooting sequence to emphasize.
:::

## Guided exercise

Build and submit a job that prints:

- a greeting
- the compute node hostname
- the current date
- the working directory

::: notes
Let participants work for a few minutes.
Circulate around directory problems and typos first; those are the most likely blockers.
Suggested time: 8 minutes.
:::

## Exercise steps

1. `mkdir practice_job`
2. `cd practice_job`
3. create `job.slurm`
4. submit with `sbatch`
5. monitor with `squeue -u $USER`
6. inspect `slurm-*.out`

Stretch goal:

Add `sleep 20` so you can observe the job in the queue or running state.

::: notes
This used to be too dense on one slide.
Now the task and the steps are separate.
:::

## Debrief: what you just did

You:

- worked safely on a login node
- created a workspace
- wrote a SLURM batch script
- requested compute resources
- submitted a job to the scheduler
- inspected the output from a compute node

That is the core beginner HPC workflow.

::: notes
This slide is important psychologically.
Name the achievement clearly.
:::

## One-slide mental summary

```text
Login node:
  prepare, edit, inspect, submit

SLURM:
  queue, schedule, allocate

Compute node:
  execute, generate output
```

If participants remember only one thing, let it be this workflow.

::: notes
Return to the core model one last time.
:::

## What comes next

Natural follow-up workshops:

- compiling and running C/C++ on LS6
- reading more detailed job output and debugging failures
- interactive jobs vs batch jobs
- MPI and OpenMP basics
- containers and reproducible workflows

This workshop maps most directly to the book chapters on:

- introduction to HPC
- Linux fundamentals
- SLURM job scheduling

::: notes
This slide connects the short workshop back to the larger curriculum.
:::

## Presenter checklist

Before teaching, verify:

- login instructions are correct
- example partition names match the real system
- module names match the real system
- workshop directories are writable
- `sbatch`, `squeue`, and `sacct` are available
- you have a backup transcript or screenshots in case live demo fails

::: notes
This is mainly for you as instructor.
Useful to keep in the deck or move to backup slides later.
:::

## Thank you

**The Take Home:** You do not need to know all of HPC to do meaningful work.
You just need one solid workflow and the confidence to run it.

Questions?
